speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start by reviewing your latest lab and vital signs from yesterday. Your fasting blood glucose was 13.7 mmol/L — that’s significantly elevated.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve been feeling unusually tired and thirsty lately, and I’ve had to urinate more often at night.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure was 135 mmHg — within acceptable range for now — and your diastolic was 73 mmHg, which is also stable.

speaker: doctor
text: That diastolic reading of 73 mmHg aligns with what we saw earlier — no concerning trends there yet.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also noticed my ankles have been a bit puffy, especially by evening.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important — we’ll assess volume status carefully. Given your labs and symptoms, we need to consider possible underlying causes beyond diabetes.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your eGFR, albuminuria, and imaging findings, we’re diagnosing Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 3, likely secondary to long-standing diabetes and hypertension.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on insulin glargine 16 units once daily at bedtime — that’s continuing for now while we adjust other aspects of your care.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, over the past week I’ve had blurred vision — it comes and goes, especially after meals.

speaker: doctor
text: Blurred vision can be a red flag for hyperglycemia-induced osmotic lens changes or early retinopathy. We’ll refer you to ophthalmology urgently for a dilated exam.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been checking my glucose at home — mostly before meals and at bedtime — and many readings are above 12 mmol/L.

speaker: doctor
text: That supports our concern. Let’s look at your most recent fasting value — did you check it this morning?

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting glucose today was 16.0 mmol/L — higher than the 13.7 we saw yesterday. That suggests worsening glycemic control despite current insulin dosing.

speaker: patient
text: I missed two doses last weekend because I felt nauseous after eating something that didn’t agree with me.

speaker: doctor
text: That could definitely contribute. We’ll review your insulin technique and timing, and consider adding rapid-acting coverage for meals if needed.

speaker: patient
text: My blood pressure has felt a bit low sometimes — like lightheaded when standing up quickly.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic pressure just now was 64 mmHg — lower than the 73 mmHg we recorded earlier this week. We’ll monitor for orthostatic changes and review your antihypertensive regimen.

speaker: patient
text: I rechecked my blood pressure at home this morning — it was back to 73 mmHg for the diastolic, same as that reading from earlier in the week.

speaker: doctor
text: Good — that reassures us the dip wasn’t sustained. Still, given your CKD and fluctuating pressures, I’m going to switch you from amlodipine to losartan 25 mg daily — it offers renal protection and better BP stability.

speaker: patient
text: Okay — and just to confirm, that 73 mmHg diastolic I got this morning matches the number you noted at the start, right?
